data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider Veradermics (MANE) After A 162% Year To Date Surge?
data.description:
• Wondering if Veradermics at around US$99 a share still lines up with the story you have in mind, or if the price is already baking in a lot of optimism.
  • The stock has seen sharp recent moves, with returns of 43% over the last 7 days, 56.8% over the last 30 days, and 162.3% year to date. This can change how you think about both risk and potential reward.
  • Recent headlines around Veradermics have focused on its position in the pharmaceuticals space and investor interest in its prospects, helping to frame these strong short term returns. This context matters because sentiment shifts can influence pricing just as much as fundamentals.
  • Even with this backdrop, Veradermics currently scores 2 out of 6 on our valuation checks, as shown by its valuation score. Next up is a closer look at how different valuation methods stack up and why there may be an even better way to judge fair value by the end of this article.

Veradermics scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Veradermics Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model projects a company’s future cash flows and then discounts them back to what they might be worth in today’s dollars. It is essentially asking what a stream of future cash flows is worth right now.

For Veradermics, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flow projections in $. The latest twelve month free cash flow is a loss of $71.63 million. Analyst and model projections then move through a period of negative free cash flows, such as $69 million in 2026 and $95.50 million in 2027, before reaching a projected free cash flow of $123 million in 2030. Beyond the analyst window, Simply Wall St extrapolates cash flows out to 2035 using its own growth assumptions.

Combining all of these discounted cash flows results in an estimated intrinsic value of about $250.72 per share. Against a current share price around $99, the DCF output implies the stock trades at roughly a 60.5% discount to this estimate, which indicates a wide gap between price and modeled value.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Veradermics is undervalued by 60.5%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 51 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Veradermics.

Approach 2: Veradermics Price vs Book

For companies in pharmaceuticals, price to book, or P/B, can be a useful yardstick because it compares what you pay in the market with the accounting value of net assets on the balance sheet. For profitable, established businesses, investors often look for a P/B that reflects both the quality of those assets and the earnings they can support.

Growth expectations and risk also shape what feels like a normal P/B level. Faster growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher multiple, while slower growth or higher uncertainty can point to a lower one. This is why simple comparisons can be tricky.

Veradermics currently trades on a P/B of 30.09x, which sits well above the Pharmaceuticals industry average of 2.63x and the peer group average of 4.33x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio is a proprietary estimate of what P/B might make sense given Veradermics’ earnings growth profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and risks. Because it pulls these company specific factors together, the Fair Ratio can be a more tailored benchmark than broad industry or peer averages.

In this case, Veradermics’ actual P/B of 30.09x is meaningfully higher than the Fair Ratio. This points to the shares screening as expensive on this measure.

Result: OVERVALUED

P/B ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Start investing in legacies, not executives. Discover our 18 top founder-led companies.

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Veradermics Narrative

Earlier it was mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation. Meet Narratives, a simple way to put a clear story behind your numbers by linking your view of a company to its forecast revenue, earnings, margins and ultimately a fair value estimate.

A Narrative connects three pieces in one place: the company story you believe, the financial forecast that story implies and the fair value you arrive at. This allows you to quickly compare that value to the current share price and decide whether the gap looks appealing or not.

Narratives are available on Simply Wall St’s Community page, used by millions of investors, and they update automatically when new information such as news, filings or earnings is added. This helps ensure your view does not stay frozen while the facts move.

For Veradermics, one investor might build a Narrative that points to a high fair value based on optimistic revenue growth and improving margins, while another might set a much lower fair value with more cautious revenue assumptions and modest profitability. Both of those views are visible and comparable against today’s Veradermics share price.

data.title: ANZ Group Holdings (ASX:ANZ) Margin Compression Puts Bullish Narratives To The Test
data.description:
ANZ Group Holdings (ASX:ANZ) has put fresh numbers on the table for H1 2026, with total revenue for the most recent half year at A$10.7 billion and basic EPS at A$0.76, set against trailing 12 month revenue of A$21.5 billion and EPS of A$1.98. Over recent halves, the bank has seen revenue move from A$10.1 billion in H2 2024 to A$11.0 billion in H1 2025 and A$10.7 billion in H2 2025, while basic EPS shifted from A$1.04 to A$1.23 and then A$0.76. This gives a clear view of how the top line and per share earnings have tracked into this result. With net profit margin over the last year at 27.4% versus 32.1% the year before, the focus now turns to how investors weigh that margin pressure against the bank's forward growth profile.

See our full analysis for ANZ Group Holdings.

With the headline figures on the table, the next step is to see how these results line up with the prevailing bull, bear and consensus narratives that have been building around ANZ over the past year.

See what the community is saying about ANZ Group Holdings

Advertisement

Margin pressure and costs in focus

  • Over the trailing 12 months, ANZ’s net profit margin is 27.4% compared with 32.1% the year before, while the cost to income ratio over the last reported period is 58.8% versus 52.2% in the prior half year where it was disclosed.
  • Consensus narrative expects efficiency gains from the Suncorp Bank acquisition and the shift to a lower cost dual platform to support margins, yet the recent rise in the cost to income ratio and the current margin level give a clear reference point for how much operating leverage those initiatives would need to offset.
    • Supporters of the bullish view point to projected synergies from Suncorp and the planned decommissioning of legacy systems from 2026 to 2028 as potential cost savings drivers, while the move from a cost to income ratio of 52.2% to 58.8% in the latest disclosed periods shows that technology and integration spending are currently weighing on efficiency.
    • At the same time, consensus commentary highlights expectations for improved net margins over time as digital and AI driven platforms scale, which sits against the reported step down in net profit margin from 32.1% to 27.4% and gives investors a concrete benchmark to test whether those future margin goals are being met.

Bulls argue that the real test for these margin numbers will be how quickly ANZ converts its platform plans and Suncorp integration into tangible cost savings and profitability gains, and that is exactly what the detailed bull case narrative unpacks in more depth. 🐂 ANZ Group Holdings Bull Case

Loan book growth and credit quality

  • Total loans reported in recent halves sit at A$803.3b in H2 2024, A$820.2b in H1 2025 and A$829.5b in H2 2025, while non performing loans over those same periods are A$1.7b, A$2.3b and A$2.5b respectively.
  • Consensus narrative frames the Suncorp acquisition and focus on Queensland as a way to build scale in lending, and these loan and non performing loan figures give a useful check on that story by showing both growing exposure and the level of stressed loans attached to it.
    • Supportive readers of the bullish case point to expected larger and earlier synergies from Suncorp as a growth driver for the loan book, and the step up from A$803.3b to A$829.5b across the last three reported halves illustrates that ANZ is already operating with a very large lending base that could benefit from any efficiency gains.
    • On the cautious side, bears focus on credit risk and regulatory scrutiny, and the movement in non performing loans from A$1.7b to A$2.5b over the same sequence of halves offers a concrete series for assessing how credit quality trends against any future macro or regulatory changes mentioned in the risk list.

data.title: Be Sure To Check Out Storytel AB (publ) (STO:STORY B) Before It Goes Ex-Dividend
data.description:
Storytel AB (publ) (STO:STORY B) is about to trade ex-dividend in the next 4 days. The ex-dividend date generally occurs two days before the record date, which is the day on which shareholders need to be on the company's books in order to receive a dividend. The ex-dividend date is of consequence because whenever a stock is bought or sold, the trade can take two business days or more to settle. Therefore, if you purchase Storytel's shares on or after the 6th of May, you won't be eligible to receive the dividend, when it is paid on the 12th of May.

The upcoming dividend for Storytel is kr01.50 per share. If you buy this business for its dividend, you should have an idea of whether Storytel's dividend is reliable and sustainable. As a result, readers should always check whether Storytel has been able to grow its dividends, or if the dividend might be cut.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Dividends are usually paid out of company profits, so if a company pays out more than it earned then its dividend is usually at greater risk of being cut. Storytel is paying out just 24% of its profit after tax, which is comfortably low and leaves plenty of breathing room in the case of adverse events.

Check out our latest analysis for Storytel

Click here to see the company's payout ratio, plus analyst estimates of its future dividends.

Advertisement

Have Earnings And Dividends Been Growing?

Businesses with strong growth prospects usually make the best dividend payers, because it's easier to grow dividends when earnings per share are improving. If earnings decline and the company is forced to cut its dividend, investors could watch the value of their investment go up in smoke. That's why it's comforting to see Storytel's earnings have been skyrocketing, up 28% per annum for the past five years. Storytel looks like a real growth company, with earnings per share growing at a cracking pace and the company reinvesting most of its profits in the business.

This is Storytel's first year of paying a regular dividend, which is exciting for shareholders - but it does mean there's no dividend history to examine.

Final Takeaway

Is Storytel worth buying for its dividend? Companies like Storytel that are growing rapidly and paying out a low fraction of earnings, are usually reinvesting heavily in their business. Perhaps even more importantly - this can sometimes signal management is focused on the long term future of the business. We think this is a pretty attractive combination, and would be interested in investigating Storytel more closely.

Earnings are an important time for investors, as they can track a company's performance, look at what the analysts are forecasting for next year, and see if there's been a change in sentiment towards the company. This would reflect a credible 4.7% increase on its revenue over the past 12 months. Statutory earnings per share are predicted to rise 9.1% to JP¥222. Before this earnings report, the analysts had been forecasting revenues of JP¥3.78t and earnings per share (EPS) of JP¥220 in 2027. So it's pretty clear that, although the analysts have updated their estimates, there's been no major change in expectations for the business following the latest results.

There were no changes to revenue or earnings estimates or the price target of JP¥6,008, suggesting that the company has met expectations in its recent result. Of course, another way to look at these forecasts is to place them into context against the industry itself. It's clear from the latest estimates that NEC's rate of growth is expected to accelerate meaningfully, with the forecast 4.7% annualised revenue growth to the end of 2027 noticeably faster than its historical growth of 3.7% p.a. over the past five years. NEC is expected to grow at about the same rate as its industry, so it's not clear that we can draw any conclusions from its growth relative to competitors.

The most important thing to take away is that there's been no major change in sentiment, with the analysts reconfirming that the business is performing in line with their previous earnings per share estimates. They also reconfirmed their revenue estimates, with the company predicted to grow at about the same rate as the wider industry. The consensus price target held steady at JP¥6,008, with the latest estimates not enough to have an impact on their price targets.

data.description:
Farmland Partners (FPI) opened 2026 with Q1 results framed by recent quarterly revenue between US$9.96 million and US$20.68 million and basic EPS ranging from a small loss of about US$0.00 to US$1.22, set against a trailing 12 month EPS of US$0.65. Over the past year, the company has seen revenue move between US$51.79 million and US$57.53 million on a trailing 12 month basis, while basic EPS has ranged from US$0.65 to US$1.45. This provides a clear view of how top line and per share earnings have tracked together. With trailing net profit margins at 55.6% compared with 98.1% a year earlier and shaped by a one off gain of US$18.6 million, these results put profit quality and margin resilience squarely in focus for investors.

FFO of US$18.3m keeps REIT cash metric in focus

  • On a trailing 12 month basis, Funds From Operations sits at US$18.3m, compared with quarterly FFO figures between US$1.3m and US$11.3m over the last six reported quarters. This highlights how REIT style cash earnings differ from the much higher net income that was lifted by the US$18.6m one off gain.
  • Consensus narrative points to stable revenue and margin support from premium farmland, and the FFO data partly backs that by showing multi quarter cash generation. Yet the contrast between US$18.3m of FFO and trailing net income of US$28.8m is a reminder that part of the reported profitability comes from non recurring items, not just ongoing farm rents and related income.
    • The consensus view links long term revenue durability to scarce high quality farmland and robust global food demand. At the same time, the wide range in quarterly FFO, from around US$1.3m to US$11.3m, shows that cash flow can still move around as dispositions and farm performance change period by period.
    • Comments about portfolio rationalisation toward Midwest farmland and capital discipline sit alongside these figures, suggesting that any assessment of long term stability needs to separate this US$18.3m FFO stream from the boost created by the US$18.6m gain included in net income.

Margins at 55.6% with an US$18.6m one off gain

  • Trailing net profit margin is 55.6%, down from 98.1% the prior year, and includes a US$18.6m one off gain that lifts reported profitability relative to what underlying operations alone would show.
  • Bears highlight risks from water driven write downs and crop specific headwinds, and the margin pattern gives that cautious view some grounding because profitability has stepped down from 98.1% to 55.6% while remaining flattered by the US$18.6m gain.
    • Concerns about regulatory driven water issues and around 50% write downs on some specialty crop farms line up with the lower margin, which is now much closer to what you might expect from a REIT that has been selling or impairing assets in challenged regions.
    • Bearish commentary about limited acquisition activity and more reliance on dispositions also ties in with the idea that high recent margins are partly transaction driven, so trailing figures may not fully represent recurring rental income strength.
On these margin figures, skeptics argue the recent profitability owes a lot to asset sales rather than repeatable rent checks, which is exactly the kind of nuance unpacked in the 🐻 Farmland Partners Bear Case.

P/E of 16.2x versus 27.9x industry level

  • FPI trades on a P/E of 16.2x compared with 27.9x for North American Specialized REITs and 22.8x for peers, while the US$10.75 share price sits above the DCF fair value of US$7.92 and below the analyst price target of US$12.50.
  • Consensus narrative talks about long term asset value support from scarce farmland and disciplined capital deployment, and the lower P/E relative to the 27.9x industry figure fits that constructive angle. Yet the share price trading above the US$7.92 DCF fair value and a dividend yield of 3.35% that is not well covered by earnings both serve as reminders that income and valuation signals are mixed rather than one sided.
    • The gap between the US$10.75 price and US$12.50 analyst target aligns with the idea that high quality farmland and portfolio focus can justify a premium to some valuation models, but it also means you are relying on those long term assumptions rather than clear near term earnings coverage of the dividend.
    • The combination of a below industry P/E, a price above DCF fair value, and weaker dividend cover encourages you to weigh whether the farmland story and asset recycling effort are strong enough to support both capital appreciation and that 3.35% yield over time.
